Job here explains what he could not justify, his desire for death. Consider people's current situation: they are on earth. They are still on earth, not in hell. Is there not a time appointed for their stay here? Yes, certainly, and the appointment is made by Him who made us and sent us here. During that time, man’s life is a struggle, and like day laborers, who have the work of the day to do in its day, and must account for it at night. Job thought he had as much reason to wish for death as a poor servant tired with his work has to wish for the shadows of evening when he will rest. The sleep of the working man is sweet; nor can any rich person take so much satisfaction in their wealth as a hired worker does in their day’s wages. The comparison is clear; hear his complaint: His days were useless, and had been so for a long time; but when we are not able to work for God, if we sit quietly for Him, we will be accepted. His nights were restless. Whatever is painful, it is good to see it appointed for us, and designed for some holy purpose. When we have comfortable nights, we must also see them appointed to us and be thankful for them. His body was repulsive. See what vile bodies we have. His life was passing quickly. While we are living, every day, like the shuttle, leaves a thread behind; many weave a spider’s web that will fail. But if, while we live, we live unto the Lord in works of faith and labors of love, we shall have the benefit, for everyone shall reap as he sows and wear what he weaves.

Plain truths about the shortness and vanity of man's life and the certainty of death do us good when we think and speak of them with application to ourselves. Dying is done only once, so it must be well done. An error here cannot be corrected. Other clouds arise, but the same cloud never returns; just as a new generation of men arises, but the former generation vanishes away. Glorified saints will not return to the cares and sorrows of their homes; nor will condemned sinners return to the gaieties and pleasures of their homes. It concerns us to secure a better place when we die. From these reasons Job might have drawn a better conclusion than this: I will complain. When we have only a few breaths left, we should spend them in the holy, gracious breathings of faith and prayer, not in the repulsive, harmful breathings of sin and corruption. We have much reason to pray that He who keeps Israel, and neither slumbers nor sleeps, may keep us when we slumber and sleep. Job desires to rest in his grave. Doubtless, this was his weakness; for though a good person would choose death rather than sin, he should be content to live as long as God pleases, because life is our opportunity to glorify Him and prepare for heaven.

Job reasons with God concerning His dealings with man. But in the midst of this discussion, Job seems to have lifted his thoughts to God with some faith and hope. Observe the concern he has about his sins. The best people have cause to complain of sin; and the better they are, the more they will complain of it. God is the Preserver of our lives and the Savior of the souls of all who believe; but probably Job meant the Observer of men, whose eyes are upon the ways and hearts of all men. We can hide nothing from Him; let us plead guilty before His throne of grace so that we may not be condemned at His judgment seat. Job maintained against his friends that he was not a hypocrite, not a wicked person, yet he acknowledges to God that he has sinned. The best must do the same before the Lord. He seriously inquires how he might be at peace with God and earnestly begs forgiveness of his sins. He means more than the removal of his outward trouble and is eager for the return of God’s favor. Wherever the Lord removes the guilt of sin, He breaks its power. To strengthen his prayer for pardon, Job pleads the prospect that he will die quickly. If my sins are not pardoned while I live, I am lost forever. How wretched is sinful man without knowledge of the Savior!
